key_points: On-campus dorms expensive with bad food; off-campus often cheaper
Dorm flooding reported in Spanton including sewage water incidents
Off-campus options like Edge (~$500/mo) exist but noise is a problem
key_quotes: Dont live in the dorms unless you need to because its expensive + roommates can suck
did anyone else have their room get flooded by doodoo water?
$500/mo is cheap as it gets. Anything walkable will take you up to $800

key_points: Widely described as a commuter school; making friends is genuinely hard
Events for commuters scheduled at times commuters literally cannot attend
LGBT community exists but campus overall feels socially quiet
key_quotes: I've found it incredibly hard to make friends honestly
Most commuters are commuters because they work and couldn't be there at 1pm
Campus is pretty quiet from what I've experienced

key_points: Food choices described as horrible by multiple students
Book bundle charges ~$350 but can include access to homework platforms
Dorms with kitchens strongly recommended to avoid dining dependency
key_quotes: The food choices are horrible so I would choose a dorm with a kitchen
It's a scammmm. I have no required text so 0 dollars. They have the nerve to tell me I'm saving 200 bucks
I always wait until the last minute to see if I really need the book bundle...I have NEVER needed it

key_points: No direct mental health support mentions; social isolation is a recurring theme
Commuter structure leaves many students feeling disconnected from campus life
Accessibility issues compound stress for students with disabilities
key_quotes: I've found it incredibly hard to make friends honestly
The elevator issues are horrible I would miss classes in kolbe because of it
I am quite nervous about the housing situation

key_points: Financial aid processing delays are frequent and poorly communicated
Scholarships must be applied before grants/loans or students lose money
Fees beyond tuition add up: ~$1200/semester in mandatory fees plus optional book bundle
key_quotes: They're finding any excuse this year. Its been 1 thing after another with mine. Found out today there will be yet ANOTHER 3 to 5 day delay
If you got any type of scholarships from the university of Akron, make sure financial aid adds their scholarships before applying any grants or student loans
My fees this year were $1200 each semester and then the optional book bundle was $350ish and then the parking pass was $280 a semester

key_points: Polymer engineering is genuinely world-class; worth attending for that alone
Program cuts and course cancellations happening without updating the website
Tutoring and professor office hours are available and generally praised
key_quotes: The department is well-known among the Polymers and material science circle worldwide
A lot of majors are being changed and aren't always being reflected online
lol what program. The building floods like 3 times a year and they cut most of it

key_points: Mass faculty layoffs happened during COVID with no corresponding admin cuts
Website redesign widely hated; navigation described as broken and inaccessible
Grad students stuck in programs for 5+ years due to departmental mismanagement
key_quotes: DO NOT go here for grad school. My cohort and I were stuck in a masters program for 5 years due to ridiculous mismanagement
Alternative title: Universities purge tenured staff to throw more money at executive staff and coaches
Hmm nothing about cutting administration or administrative salaries though

key_points: Construction disrupting central campus pathways for extended periods mid-semester
Polsky building requires dangerous road crossings; students report near-misses
City safety concerns are overblown per students, but off-campus incidents do occur
key_quotes: I have almost been hit several times walking to polsky just this semester alone
A homeless/drunk guy walked up to me and said 'come here little man' and started following me. This was during daylight
Akron campus is super safe. Never felt in danger, there's usually campus and Akron police roaming around

key_points: Polymer engineering grads well-positioned for industry careers nationally
Political science has strong local government connections via Bliss Institute
Art and some humanities programs have been quietly cut, hurting career prep
key_quotes: The program will set you up for a successful career in industry or (to a lesser extent) in academia
UA has an excellent PoliSci program with well-known faculty and great connections/involvement with local government
This is what happens at Akron with management. The art school was thriving when I attended

key_points: Cheaper cost of attendance than Toledo and many Ohio peers; scholarships are competitive
Hidden fees ($1200/semester mandatory + $350 book bundle + $280 parking) add up fast
Parking managed by predatory third-party company with arbitrary ticketing
key_quotes: Akron already has a cheaper cost of attendance by 3000 dollars
iParq is a private company that exists solely to profit off of people doing things minorly wrong
In overall terms, from my experience Akron is pretty good compared to how much you pay

key_points: Commuter school energy dominates; residential students have a better time socially
Campus has quirky, low-key charm but lacks consistent school spirit
Students who engage actively find community; passive attendees feel invisible
key_quotes: The thing about University in general is that it is 1000% what you make of it
I transferred to akron from a community college so I mean I'm used to useless events
went to akron my freshman year, i LOVED IT!

key_points: **Administration:** faculty mass-fired during COVID; no admin salary cuts made
**Infrastructure:** Polsky building has broken elevators, flooding, and dangerous crosswalks
**Programs:** courses listed online are being cancelled without updating official catalog
key_quotes: DO NOT go here for grad school. My cohort and I were stuck in a masters program for 5 years due to ridiculous mismanagement
lol what program. The building floods like 3 times a year and they cut most of it
I have almost been hit several times walking to polsky just this semester alone

key_points: Polymer science program is elite and globally recognized in its field
Bliss Institute of Applied Politics offers rare hands-on government connections
Off-campus housing near Wheeler St can be as low as $350-450/month with utilities
key_quotes: One note, if a polymer major is your goal, Akron has been one of the preeminent programs in polymer research
Akron's political science programs are fantastic with great connections to local government
I lived on Wheeler & Sherman for $500/mo with utilities included
